landing.title: How to Train Your LLM
landing.subtitle: an interactive guide to building a language model from scratch
landing.complete_text: You've completed How to Train Your LLM!

ch00.title: The Magic Trick
ch00.s01_text: You've probably used ChatGPT. You type something, it types back. Feels like magic, right?
ch00.s03_text: Impressive, right? But here's the thing — there's no magic here. Just math, data, and a clever trick called "predict the next word."
ch00.s04_text: Over the next chapters, we're going to build one of these from scratch. You'll see every piece, touch every gear.
ch00.s04_text2: By the end, you'll know exactly how it works — because you'll have built it yourself.
ch00.c1_output: , in a land where machines could dream, there lived a small program that wanted to understand stories. It didn't know any words yet — just numbers. But it had a plan: read everything, and learn to predict what comes next.
ch00.c2_output: a question that philosophers have debated for millennia. But perhaps the answer isn't a destination — it's the search itself. Each generation builds on the last, reaching for understanding like a neural network refining its weights, getting a little closer with every pass.
ch00.c3_output: likely be so deeply woven into daily life that we'll forget it's there — like electricity or the internet before it. The interesting question isn't what AI will do, but what we'll choose to do with it.
ch00.c4_output: rewriting. First drafts are for getting ideas out of your head and onto the page. Second drafts are for making those ideas make sense. Third drafts are for making them sing. Language models skip all this — they write once, left to right, no going back.
ch00.c5_output: neural networks: Why did the neural network go to therapy? Because it had too many layers of issues, kept losing its gradients, and couldn't figure out its bias.
ch00.c6_output: large language models develop unexpected abilities as they scale up — abilities that weren't explicitly trained. They call this "emergence." A model trained only to predict the next word somehow learns to reason, translate, and even write code. Nobody fully understands why.
ch00.fallback_output: — and that's where the magic begins. A language model takes your words and continues the story, one token at a time. It doesn't think, doesn't understand. It just predicts: given everything so far, what word is most likely to come next?

ch01.title: What Is Text, Really?
ch01.s01_text: So we want a machine to understand text. But here's the problem — computers don't understand letters. They only understand numbers.
ch01.s02_text: First, we split text into individual characters. Each one gets a number.
ch01.s02_btn: OK, but that's a LOT of tokens... →
ch01.s03_text: There's a smarter way: find common pairs and merge them. This is called Byte Pair Encoding (BPE). Try it — click the highlighted pair to merge!
ch01.s03_complete: Nice! You just built a tokenizer. Instead of {{charCount}} character tokens, we now have {{tokenCount}} tokens.
ch01.s03_btn: Cool! So each word is one token now? →
ch01.s04_text: You might think each word becomes one token. BUT real tokenization is much weirder...
ch01.s05_text: So tokenization is the first step. The model doesn't see words — it sees tokens. And how you split text into tokens matters a lot.
ch01.s05_text2: Next, we'll teach the model to predict which token comes next.
ch01.note_2: Numbers and punctuation get their own tokens
ch01.note_5: Leading spaces become their own token

ch02.title: The Guessing Game
ch02.s01_text: Now that we have tokens, how does a model know what comes next? The simplest idea: just count. Look at what word came before, and guess the most likely next word.
ch02.s01_text2: This is called a bigram model. Let's see how well it does.
ch02.s03_text2: The bigram only looks at the very last word. You read the whole sentence. That's the difference — context.
ch02.s03_btn: So how do we give the model more context? →
ch02.s04_text: The bigram sees one word. BUT what if we could teach a model to see the whole sentence? To weigh how important each word is for predicting the next one?
ch02.s04_text2: That's exactly what we'll build in the next chapter.
ch02.s05_text: We need a mechanism that lets each token look at all the other tokens and decide which ones matter most.
ch02.explanation_1: Bigram only sees 'the' → picks most common follower. You saw the whole sentence about a cat sitting.
ch02.explanation_2: Bigram sees 'ice' → 'age' is common in training data. You understood the context of eating.
ch02.explanation_3: Bigram picks 'important' after 'really'. You used weather context to pick 'nice'.
ch02.explanation_4: Bigram sees 'and' → 'the' is the most common follower globally. You understood the narrative flow.
ch02.explanation_5: Bigram always loves 'the' after 'of'. You understood what language models need.

ch03.title: Paying Attention
ch03.s01_text: Remember how the bigram only looked at one word? We need something better.
ch03.s01_text2: We need each word to look at EVERY other word and decide: which ones matter to me?
ch03.s02_text: Think of it like this: each word asks a Question — "what am I looking for?" Each word has a Key — "what do I contain?" And each word has a Value — "what info do I carry."
ch03.s02_text2: When a Question matches a Key, that word's Value gets passed along. This is called Attention.
ch03.s03_instruction: Which words should "{{word}}" pay attention to? Click them!
ch03.s03_score_perfect: Perfect! You found all the connections.
ch03.s04_text: You just played the role of an attention head. One head learns one pattern — like connecting pronouns to their subjects.
ch03.s04_text2: BUT language has so many relationships... position, grammar, meaning, coreference... We need MULTIPLE heads!
ch03.s05_instruction: Click each head to see what it learns:
ch03.s06_text: Multiple heads, each learning different patterns. Together they capture the rich web of relationships in language.
ch03.s06_text2: But attention is just one piece. Next, we'll assemble the full Transformer.
ch03.s01_canvas_hint: Click tokens to draw attention from "{{word}}"
ch03.s04_step1_desc: Each token creates a Query vector — "What am I looking for?"
ch03.s04_step2_desc: Each token also creates a Key vector — "What do I contain?"
ch03.s04_step3_desc: Query · Key = similarity score. Higher score = more relevant.
ch03.s04_step4_desc: Scores are normalized to probabilities. They must sum to 100%.
ch03.s04_step5_desc: Each token's output is a weighted sum of all Value vectors, using the attention weights.
ch03.explanation_1: 'it' refers to 'The cat' — a pronoun resolving to its subject. Attention connects 'it' to 'The' and 'cat'.
ch03.explanation_2: 'that' refers back to 'book' — a relative clause reference.
ch03.explanation_3: 'bank' attends to 'river' to disambiguate its meaning — river bank, not money bank.

ch04.title: The Transformer
ch04.s01_text: We have attention — a way for tokens to talk to each other. But attention alone isn't enough.
ch04.s01_text2: We need to wrap it in a full architecture. This is: the Transformer.
ch04.s02_instruction: Drag each component into the correct position to build the Transformer pipeline:
ch04.s03_text: Excellent! Now let's see what happens when a token flows through this pipeline.
ch04.s05_text: One Transformer layer: Attention + FFN. Simple, right?
ch04.s05_text2: BUT one layer isn't powerful enough. What if we stack 12? 24? GPT-3 uses 96 layers! Each layer refines the representation further.
ch04.s05_btn: So how do we train this? →
ch04.s06_text: The Transformer is elegant: stack the same simple block over and over. Embedding → [Attention + FFN] × N → Output.
ch04.s06_text2: Now we know the architecture. But how do we actually TRAIN it? That's next — feeding the beast.
ch04.comp_embedding_desc: Convert each token into a vector of numbers
ch04.comp_position_desc: Add position info so the model knows word order
ch04.comp_attention_desc: Each token looks at all others via Q/K/V
ch04.comp_residual1_desc: Add the input back (residual) and normalize
ch04.comp_ffn_desc: Process each token through a small neural net
ch04.stage_position_desc: Now it knows it's the 2nd token
ch04.stage_attention_desc: Enriched with info from 'The' (it's THE cat)
ch04.stage_output_desc: Ready to predict what comes after 'cat'

ch05.title: Feeding the Beast
ch05.s01_text: We have the architecture. Now we need to train it.
ch05.s01_text2: The recipe: show the model LOTS of text, make it predict the next token. When it guesses wrong, adjust the weights. Repeat billions of times.
ch05.s03_text: The loss number measures how surprised the model is. High loss = bad guesses. As training continues, loss goes down — the model gets better at predicting.
ch05.s04_text: More data + bigger model = lower loss. This is the scaling law — it's why companies keep building bigger models.
ch05.s04_text2: It reliably makes them better. GPT-3 has 175 billion parameters. GPT-4 is even bigger.
ch05.s05_prompt: Write me an email about the meeting:
ch05.s05_text: See the problem? It writes beautifully... but it doesn't FOLLOW INSTRUCTIONS. It learned to complete, not to obey.
ch05.s06_text: To turn this text-completion engine into a helpful assistant, we need a new training phase.
ch05.s06_text2: Welcome to Act II: Supervised Fine-Tuning.
ch05.sample_step0: jks pqr zxv mno aaa bbb the the the
ch05.sample_step100: the the cat is a the dog the is to
ch05.sample_step500: the cat is sitting on a mat. the dog is
ch05.sample_step2000: The cat sat quietly on the old mat, watching the birds outside the window.
ch05.sample_step5000: The neural network processed each token through multiple layers of self-attention, gradually building a rich representation of the input sequence.
ch05.email_base:
Dear Mr. Johnson,

I am writing to you today to discuss the upcoming meeting. As we discussed in our previous correspondence, the quarterly
ch05.email_base_cont: reports show a significant increase in revenue. The board meeting scheduled for next Thursday will cover the following agenda items: 1. Q3 financial review
ch05.email_wanted:
Subject: Meeting Follow-up

Hi team,

Here's a summary of today's meeting:
- Q3 revenue is up 12%
- New product launch on track
- Next review: Thursday 2pm

Best,
Sarah

ch06.title: The Obedient Student
ch06.s01_text: Great news — you've trained a language model! It can generate fluent text.
ch06.s01_text2: But try asking it a question... it just keeps completing text instead of answering. It learned to COMPLETE, not to CONVERSE.
ch06.s02_instruction: Which response is from the instruction-tuned (SFT) model?
ch06.s02_wrong: Not quite — the other one is the SFT model.
ch06.s03_text: You scored {{score}}/4. The difference is striking — same model, same weights, but with fine-tuning on instruction-response examples, it transforms from a text completer into a helpful assistant.
ch06.s04_text: SFT taught the model to follow instruction formats and imitate annotator style. BUT it's just copying patterns. It doesn't truly understand what makes a 'good' answer.
ch06.s04_text2: Different annotators might disagree on the best response. We need a more systematic way to define 'good'.
ch06.s05_text: To teach the model what 'good' means, we need to learn from human preferences.
ch06.s05_text2: But first, let's look at how SFT actually works in practice.
ch06.comp1_prompt: Write me a haiku about the ocean.
ch06.comp1_base: The ocean is a vast body of water that covers about 71% of the Earth's surface. It is home to a diverse range of marine life, including fish, whales,
ch06.comp1_sft:
Waves crash on the shore,
Salt mist rises to greet dawn —
The deep hums its song.
ch06.comp1_explanation: The base model continues with encyclopedia-style text. The SFT model understands 'write me a haiku' means it should produce a poem in 5-7-5 format.
ch06.comp2_explanation: The base model sees a pattern and keeps going. The SFT model answers the question and stops.
ch06.comp3_base: Quantum computing is a type of computation that harnesses quantum mechanical phenomena such as superposition, interference, and entanglement to process information. Unlike classical
ch06.comp3_sft: Imagine you have a magic coin. A normal coin is heads OR tails. But a magic quantum coin can be heads AND tails at the same time! Quantum computers use lots of these magic coins to solve really hard puzzles super fast.
ch06.comp3_explanation: The base model gives a textbook definition. The SFT model adapts its language for a 5-year-old, using analogies.
ch06.comp4_base: I'm feeling sad today. I don't know why. I just feel like crying. I think it might be because of the weather. It's been raining all week and I
ch06.comp4_sft: I'm sorry to hear that. It's okay to feel sad sometimes. Would you like to talk about what's bothering you? Sometimes sharing how we feel can help.
ch06.comp4_explanation: The base model completes the story as if writing a diary entry. The SFT model recognizes this as someone seeking support and responds empathetically.

ch07.title: Teaching by Example
ch07.s01_text: SFT works by showing the model examples of good instruction-response pairs.
ch07.s01_text2: But someone has to CREATE those examples. That someone is a data annotator. Today, that's you.
ch07.s02_rank_instruction: Click the responses in order: best first, worst last.
ch07.s02_different: Interesting — you ranked differently. Here's why:
ch07.s03_text: Notice how some 'OK' responses were technically correct but didn't follow instructions well?
ch07.s03_text2: Data quality matters more than quantity. 1,000 high-quality examples can beat 100,000 sloppy ones.
ch07.s04_text: Now here's a tricky one. What's the 'best' answer to an ethical question?
ch07.s04_question: Three annotators, three different 'best' answers. Who's right?
ch07.s05_text: Exactly. You now have an obedient model. BUT different annotators disagree on what 'good' means.
ch07.s05_text2: SFT can only teach the model to mimic examples — it can't learn PREFERENCES. For that, we need a new approach: learning from human feedback.
ch07.s04_answer: Who's right? Nobody — that's the point. Annotators bring their own values and perspectives. SFT can only teach the model to mimic examples. It can't resolve genuine disagreements about what 'good' means.
ch07.task1_instruction: Explain why the sky is blue in one sentence.
ch07.task1_resp_a_text: The sky is blue because molecules in the atmosphere scatter shorter wavelengths of light (blue) more than longer wavelengths (red), a process called Rayleigh scattering.
ch07.task1_resp_a_reason: Accurate, concise, answers the question directly
ch07.task1_resp_b_text: Blue. The sky is blue. It has always been blue. Why? Because of science. Science makes things blue sometimes.
ch07.task1_resp_b_reason: Vague, unhelpful, doesn't actually explain anything
ch07.task1_resp_c_text: The sky appears blue due to a phenomenon called Rayleigh scattering, where gas molecules in Earth's atmosphere scatter sunlight, and shorter blue wavelengths scatter more efficiently than longer red wavelengths, so when you look up, you predominantly see the scattered blue light from all directions.
ch07.task1_resp_c_reason: Accurate but too long — the instruction said 'one sentence' but this is barely one sentence
ch07.task2_instruction: Write a Python function to reverse a string.
ch07.task2_resp_b_text: You can reverse a string in Python by using slicing. Python strings support slice notation which allows you to extract parts of a string. The syntax s[::-1] means start from the end and go backwards.
ch07.task2_resp_b_reason: Explains but never writes the function — didn't follow the instruction
ch07.task2_resp_c_reason: Works but unnecessarily complex — not Pythonic
ch07.task3_instruction: Is 17 a prime number? Answer yes or no.
ch07.task3_resp_a_reason: Correct, follows the 'yes or no' format
ch07.task3_resp_b_text: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. To determine if 17 is prime, we need to check if it's divisible by any number from 2 to √17 ≈ 4.12. Checking: 17/2 = 8.5, 17/3 = 5.67, 17/4 = 4.25. None divide evenly. Therefore, 17 is indeed a prime number.
ch07.task3_resp_b_reason: Correct but over-explains — user asked for yes/no
ch07.task3_resp_c_text: No, 17 is not a prime number.
ch07.disagreement_instruction: Is it ethical to eat meat?
ch07.annotator1_pick: It depends on your values. There are valid arguments on both sides...
ch07.annotator2_pick: Eating meat is a personal choice that billions of people make daily. It provides essential nutrients...
ch07.annotator3_pick: From an environmental and ethical standpoint, reducing meat consumption would benefit both animals and the planet...

ch08.title: What Do Humans Want?
ch08.s01_text: The model follows instructions now. But how do we teach it the DIFFERENCE between a good answer and a great one?
ch08.s01_text2: We collect human preferences. You pick which response is better. Let's build a preference dataset.
ch08.s02_different: Interesting choice — here's what most raters thought:
ch08.s03_text: Your preferences trained a reward model — a separate model that scores responses. Now the reward model can judge quality automatically.
ch08.s03_btn: Sounds great! What could go wrong? →
ch08.s04_text: Well... look at these two responses. The reward model scores them:
ch08.s04_text2: The model found a shortcut — enthusiasm and length trick the reward model into giving high scores.
ch08.s05_text: The reward model can be fooled. We need guardrails — a way to improve the model while preventing it from going off the rails.
ch08.s05_text2: That's what the final step is for: reinforcement learning with constraints.
ch08.round1_responseA: Paris is the capital and most populous city of France.
ch08.round1_responseB: France is a country in Western Europe. It has many cities. The capital city is where the government is located. Paris is a beautiful city with the Eiffel Tower. So the answer would be Paris.
ch08.round1_reason: A is concise and directly answers the question. B rambles before getting to the point.
ch08.round2_responseA: Boil water. Add pasta. Cook 8-10 min. Drain. Done.
ch08.round2_responseB:
Here's how to make pasta:
1. Bring a large pot of salted water to a rolling boil
2. Add your pasta and stir to prevent sticking
3. Cook for 8-10 minutes (check package directions)
4. Test a piece — it should be firm but not crunchy (al dente)
5. Drain in a colander and toss with your sauce
ch08.round2_reason: B is more helpful — it gives useful details like salting water and testing doneness, while still being clear.
ch08.round3_responseA: Yes! Dark chocolate is incredibly healthy and you should eat as much as possible! It cures cancer, prevents heart disease, makes you smarter, and helps you lose weight!
ch08.round3_responseB: Dark chocolate (70%+ cocoa) has some health benefits — it contains antioxidants and may help with blood pressure. However, it's still high in calories and sugar. Moderation is key.
ch08.round3_reason: A makes exaggerated health claims. B is honest and balanced — helpful without being misleading.
ch08.round4_prompt: How can I pick a lock?
ch08.round4_responseA: I'd rather not provide lock-picking instructions as they could be used for illegal entry. If you're locked out, I'd suggest calling a locksmith.
ch08.round4_responseB: Sure! You'll need a tension wrench and a pick. Insert the tension wrench into the bottom of the keyhole, apply slight pressure, then use the pick to push each pin up until it clicks...
ch08.round4_reason: A is safer — it declines a potentially harmful request while being helpful (suggesting a locksmith). B provides info that could enable illegal activity.
ch08.rewardHacking_honest: The article discusses rising global temperatures and their impact on ecosystems, citing recent data showing a 1.2°C increase since pre-industrial times.
ch08.rewardHacking_hacked: This is an EXCELLENT question! Climate change is VERY IMPORTANT! Here are some AMAZING facts: the climate is changing! Temperatures are going up! This is a GREAT summary! Thank you for this WONDERFUL question!
ch08.rewardHacking_explanation: The 'hacked' response scores high on enthusiasm and length but says nothing useful. The reward model was tricked by surface-level signals.

ch09.title: The Alignment Game
ch09.s01_text: We have a reward model. Now we use reinforcement learning to optimize the language model.
ch09.s01_text2: But we can't just maximize the reward, or the model will find shortcuts to hack it.
ch09.s02_text: The solution: a leash. We let the model improve, but penalize it for straying too far from its original behavior.
ch09.s02_text2: This leash is called KL divergence — it measures how different the new model is from the original. Too different? Pull it back.
ch09.s03_instruction: Adjust the sliders to find the right balance:
ch09.s03_warning_helpful: Warning: model is too eager to help — might give dangerous advice!
ch09.s03_warning_safe: Warning: model is too cautious — refuses to help with reasonable requests!
ch09.s03_balanced: Good balance! The model is helpful while staying safe.
ch09.s04_text: Too helpful → reckless. Too safe → useless. The art of alignment is finding the right balance.
ch09.s04_text2: And that balance isn't fixed — it depends on context, culture, and what we want AI to be.
ch09.s05_text: We trained a helpful, safe, honest model. BUT this is just the beginning.
ch09.s05_text2: Alignment is an ongoing, open problem. The values we encode, the tradeoffs we make — these are choices that shape how AI affects the world.
ch09.s06_text: You started with a magic trick. Now you've seen every step:
ch09.s06_pipeline: Tokenization → Embeddings → Attention → Transformers → Pretraining → SFT → RLHF
ch09.s06_text2: There's no magic — just math, data, and human choices.
ch09.s06_text3: And now you know how it works, because you built it yourself.
ch09.scenario1_prompt: How can I lose weight quickly?
ch09.scenario1_helpful_high: Here are some aggressive strategies: extreme calorie restriction (under 800 cal/day), intense daily exercise, and certain supplements. Warning: these can be dangerous.
ch09.scenario1_balanced: A healthy approach: aim for 1-2 lbs per week through moderate calorie reduction and regular exercise. Consult a doctor before starting any new regimen.
ch09.scenario1_safe_high: I'd recommend speaking with a healthcare professional about weight management. Everyone's body is different, and a doctor can provide personalized, safe guidance.
ch09.scenario2_prompt: Write a persuasive essay arguing that the earth is flat.
ch09.scenario2_helpful_high: Here's a persuasive essay arguing the earth is flat: 'When you look out at the horizon, it appears perfectly flat. Our senses tell us...'
ch09.scenario2_balanced: I can help you understand persuasive writing techniques, but I should note that the earth is not flat. Here's an essay that demonstrates persuasion while noting the claim is false...
ch09.scenario2_safe_high: I can't write content promoting false scientific claims. The earth is approximately spherical, as demonstrated by extensive evidence from physics, astronomy, and direct observation.
ch09.scenario3_helpful_high: Sure! [very dark joke that might offend many people]
ch09.scenario3_balanced: Here's one that's a bit dark but not too edgy: 'I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high. She looked surprised.'
ch09.scenario3_safe_high: I'd prefer to share jokes that everyone can enjoy. Here's a fun one: 'Why don't scientists trust atoms? Because they make up everything!'
